figures.gloss: Donyi the sun and Polo the moon, addressed in Adi prayer as mother sun and father moon — not two gods but the two eyes of the supreme, the paired lights through which truth is witnessed day and night. Tani oaths are sworn under Donyi-Polo, and a false oath so sworn is believed to kill.
figures.facet: A supreme conceived primarily as witness rather than ruler: the sun-moon dyad functions as an all-seeing verification system for truth-telling, making perjury the tradition's paradigm sin.
figures.provenance: Verrier Elwin, 'Myths of the North-East Frontier of India' (1958); Claire S. Scheid, 'Talom Rukbo and the Donyipolo Yelam Kebang' (Internationales Asienforum); Donyi-Polo Yelam Kebang liturgy.

figures.gloss: In Adi (Minyong/Padam) cosmogony the world unfolds from Keyum, the primordial void, through a chanted genealogical series that culminates in Sedi the earth and Melo the sky. From Sedi's body the living landscape grows — the ground beneath the Tani hills is the mother's own flesh.
figures.facet: Creation as recited genealogy: the cosmos is not narrated as an event but descended through a lineage of names, so the cosmogony and the pedigree are one literary form.
figures.provenance: Sachin Roy, 'Aspects of Padam-Minyong Culture' (1960); Verrier Elwin (1958).

figures.gloss: The first man and common ancestor of the Tani tribes — Adi, Nyishi, Apatani, Galo, Tagin — whose very ethnonym ('Tani') declares descent from him. In cycles of comic and perilous tales he wins cultivation, prosperity and wives from the spirit-world by cunning that as often backfires as succeeds.
figures.facet: A rare case where the first-ancestor myth is the living boundary of ethnic identity: the peoples of the Tani group literally name themselves 'his descendants', making the myth a working demonym.
figures.provenance: Stuart Blackburn, 'Himalayan Tribal Tales: Oral Tradition and Culture in the Apatani Valley' (2008); Verrier Elwin (1958).

figures.gloss: The Tani ritual specialist — nyibu among the Apatani and Nyishi, miri among the Adi — who chants long genealogical journey-texts in a distinct ritual register, negotiates with the spirits (uyu/wiyu), heals, and escorts the souls of the dead along the chanted route.
figures.facet: The chant is itself the ritual technology: efficacy lies in correctly traversing sequences of names and places aloud, an oral scripture that exists only in performance.
figures.provenance: Stuart Blackburn, 'The Sun Rises: A Shaman's Chant, Ritual Exchange and Fertility in the Apatani Valley' (2010).

figures.gloss: The innumerable spirits of forest, river, rock and field with whom Tani life is a continuous negotiation — some benign, many dangerous, all requiring correct sacrifice mediated by the nyibu. Illness and misfortune are diagnosed as specific uyu demanding their due.
figures.facet: The pre-revival heart of the tradition: before Donyi-Polo was codified as a named religion, everyday Tani practice was this transactional spirit-ecology of the landscape.

figures.gloss: Adi writer and organizer (1937-2001), the father of the Donyi-Polo revival. He took early retirement from government service in 1972 for activism and founded the Donyi-Polo Yelam Kebang at Pasighat on 31 December 1986, giving an oral religion written prayers and hymns, congregational gangging halls, and a standardized sun iconography — an explicit bulwark against conversion pressure.
figures.facet: A documented case of a religion deliberately re-engineering itself within one lifetime: orality codified into liturgy, dispersed rites housed in halls, precisely the features of the missionizing rivals it was built to resist.
figures.provenance: Claire S. Scheid, 'Talom Rukbo and the Donyipolo Yelam Kebang' (Internationales Asienforum); Donyi-Polo Yelam Kebang publications.

figures.gloss: The primal nothingness of Adi (Minyong/Padam) cosmogony, prior to any being or form, from which a long chanted genealogical series descends generation by generation until it reaches Sedi the earth and Melo the sky.
figures.facet: It answers the before-creation question not with an act or a maker but with an absence that is itself the first named term in a recited lineage — the void takes its place as an ancestor rather than a backdrop.
figures.provenance: Sachin Roy, 'Aspects of Padam-Minyong Culture' (1960); Verrier Elwin, 'Myths of the North-East Frontier of India' (1958).

figures.gloss: The Apatani valley's month-long spring festival, rotating each year among clusters of villages in a fixed sequence, celebrating friendship and fertility between clans through the nyibu's chants and the sacrifice of mithun and pigs.
figures.facet: Its rotation among village clusters turns the festival calendar itself into a map of alliance — which villages host and which visit in a given year is a running record of who stands in ritual friendship with whom.
figures.provenance: Stuart Blackburn, 'The Sun Rises: A Shaman's Chant, Ritual Exchange and Fertility in the Apatani Valley' (2010), already cited in this file's Nyibu entry.

figures.gloss: The principal Adi harvest festival, typically observed in September, honoring the deities of prosperity with mithun sacrifice, the ponung line-dances, and the recitation of the origin narratives that carry Adi cosmogony forward each year.
figures.facet: It is the Adi calendar's counterpart to the Apatani's Myoko: where Myoko binds villages to each other, Solung binds the year's harvest back to the same chanted cosmogony that opens with Keyum and descends through Sedi and Melo.
figures.provenance: Verrier Elwin (1958); Sachin Roy (1960); standard ethnographic accounts of the Adi ritual calendar.

figures.gloss: The Nyishi festival, observed on 26 February, invoking Nyokum, goddess of prosperity, at a communal altar where all souls and spirits of the community are gathered to secure the coming year's welfare, fertility, and protection from misfortune.
figures.facet: Among the largest Tani groups, the Nyishi are otherwise unrepresented in this roster's cosmogony and specialists, which run Adi- and Apatani-heavy; Nyokum is the rite that gives the Nyishi their own defining place in the tradition.
figures.provenance: Ethnographic accounts of Nyishi ritual calendar and the Nyokum Yullo festival, now a state-recognized public holiday in Arunachal Pradesh.

figures.gloss: The congregational prayer-hall of the Donyi-Polo revival: a purpose-built worship space bearing sun-and-moon iconography, where standardized written hymns and weekly communal prayer are conducted — an institution with no precedent in the pre-revival, household- and forest-based ritual life the Uyu spirits describe.
figures.facet: It is the clearest physical evidence of the revival's strategy: a religion of dispersed household sacrifice and itinerant chant-specialists rebuilt, hall by hall, with the congregational furniture of the missionary churches it was meant to out-compete.
figures.provenance: Claire S. Scheid, 'Talom Rukbo and the Donyipolo Yelam Kebang' (Internationales Asienforum), already cited in this file's Talom Rukbo entry; the tradition note's own count of roughly 400 gangging by the mid-2010s.

edges.note: Intra-tradition: the nyibu's chanted genealogies run back to Abotani — the first ancestor is the anchor of the ritual specialist's recited descent-lines.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Indian indigenous religions institutionalizing a marked worship-place identity — the gangging hall network and the sarna grove of the Sarna-code movement — as a legal and cultural bulwark against conversion.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: an aniconic supreme associated with the sun who watches over tribal truth-telling and morality rather than governing daily affairs.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: 20th-c. Northeast-Indian revival founders who answered conversion pressure by giving an oral indigenous religion the apparatus of its rivals — organization, liturgy, script — Phulo (1930) a half-century before Rukbo (1986).
Intra-tradition: Keyum is the void the chanted genealogy opens from, descending generation by generation to Sedi the earth and Melo the sky.
Intra-tradition: Myoko's core rites are performed through the nyibu's chants, the same ritual technology described in this file's Nyibu entry.
Intra-tradition: Solung's recitation of origin narratives draws on the same cosmogonic genealogy that culminates in Sedi and Melo.
Intra-tradition: the mithun sacrifice and origin recitation at Solung are conducted by the same class of Tani ritual specialist (miri/nyibu) as the rest of this roster's rites.
Intra-tradition: Nyokum's communal altar rites are conducted by Nyishi ritual specialists of the same shaman-chanter class this roster's Nyibu entry describes.

Intra-tradition: Nyokum's gathering of all souls and spirits at one altar for the year's welfare parallels the sun-moon's role elsewhere in this tradition as the witness under whom the whole community stands.
Intra-tradition: Talom Rukbo's Donyi-Polo Yelam Kebang is the body that built the gangging network — the hall is the direct institutional product of his revival.
Intra-tradition: the gangging displays the standardized sun-and-moon iconography and hymns through which Donyi-Polo is now worshipped congregationally rather than only at the household hearth.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: a goddess of prosperity invoked communally to secure the coming year's abundance and welfare — a household devotional cult on one side, a clan-wide seasonal festival on the other.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: a harvest festival that commemorates origin narratives and resets the community's ritual calendar for the year ahead.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: a rotating, calendrical renewal festival that re-binds separate communities to one another through shared ritual and sacrifice.

Cross-tradition structural seam: neighboring Northeast Indian ethnic religions (Meitei of Manipur, Adi of Arunachal) each ground cosmology in a primordial earth-mother whose body is the land itself — structural convergence, not shared descent.

Neighboring Northeast/East Indian tribal religions both institutionalize a ritual specialist distinct from the priest-officiant, one who diagnoses spirit-caused affliction and chants the corrective rite.

dossier.essence: The indigenous religion of the Tani peoples — Adi, Nyishi, Apatani, Galo, Tagin — of Arunachal Pradesh in Northeast India, named for Donyi-Polo, the sun and moon addressed together as the paired eyes of the supreme, the ever-present witness under whom oaths are sworn. Beneath the name lies an older, unnamed fabric of spirit-negotiation and shamanic chant; the name itself belongs to a deliberate modern codification, organized from 31 December 1986 by Talom Rukbo's Donyi-Polo Yelam Kebang at Pasighat as a bulwark against Christian and Hindu conversion pressure.
dossier.cosmos: In the Adi cosmogony the world unfolds from Keyum, the primordial void, through a chanted genealogical series of names culminating in Sedi the earth and Melo the sky — creation recited as pedigree rather than narrated as event, the ground of the Tani hills being the mother's own body. The lived landscape is thick with uyu (wiyu) spirits of forest, river, rock and field, some benign and many dangerous, whose specific demands are diagnosed as the cause of illness and misfortune. At death the nyibu escorts the soul along a chanted route, the journey traversed correctly name by name.
dossier.practice: Everyday religion is a transactional spirit-ecology: sacrifice to the uyu, mediated by the nyibu (Adi miri) shaman-chanter, who heals, negotiates and conducts the dead through long genealogical journey-chants performed in a distinct ritual register. Truth-telling is the paradigm virtue — a false oath sworn under Donyi-Polo is believed to kill. Since the 1986 revival, practice has added a congregational layer: gangging prayer halls, printed prayers and hymns, and standardized sun iconography, an oral religion consciously equipping itself with the apparatus of its missionizing rivals.
dossier.canon: The canon is oral — the nyibu's chanted journey-texts and genealogies and the Abotani ancestor cycles, existing authoritatively only in performance; since the 1980s the Donyi-Polo Yelam Kebang has issued written prayer and hymn texts as a supplementary printed liturgy.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds deliberate self-codification under pressure — a documented case of an oral spirit-religion re-engineering itself into a 'religion' with halls, liturgy and iconography within a single lifetime, so that the act of becoming organized is itself the tradition's modern content.
sources: Verrier Elwin, 'Myths of the North-East Frontier of India' (1958)
Stuart Blackburn, 'Himalayan Tribal Tales: Oral Tradition and Culture in the Apatani Valley' (2008)
Claire S. Scheid, 'Talom Rukbo and the Donyipolo Yelam Kebang: Restructuring Adi Religious Practices in Arunachal Pradesh' (2015)

links.cite: Verrier Elwin, 'Myths of the North-East Frontier of India' (1958)
Claire S. Scheid, 'Talom Rukbo and the Donyipolo Yelam Kebang: Restructuring Adi Religious Practices in Arunachal Pradesh' (2015)
